title: Trump’s New Tariffs: What to Know | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: CFR president Michael Froman argues the Trump administration is rebuilding, via Section 122/232/301 authorities, roughly the same global tariff wall the Supreme Court struck down under IEEPA, but hard data shows no gains yet in manufacturing's share of GDP, jobs, or reshoring construction — meaning tariffs are imposing clear near-term costs for uncertain, delayed benefits.

themes.theme: Tariffs Return Through Other Legal Channels

insights.claim: After the Supreme Court struck down Trump's sweeping global IEEPA tariffs in February 2026, the ruling proved only a brief reprieve: the administration is rebuilding equivalent tariff levels using Section 122, 232, and 301 authorities, which are slower to invoke but rest on firmer legal footing.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Investors should not treat the SCOTUS IEEPA ruling as a durable tariff off-ramp; expect tariff levels to converge back toward pre-ruling levels via alternate statutory routes.
insights.locator: In February, when the Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump’s sweeping global tariffs

insights.claim: Froman describes the administration's approach as a 'brick-by-brick reconstruction of the great tariff wall' that is nearing completion, meaning global tariff levels are converging back toward pre-ruling IEEPA levels regardless of the Supreme Court's decision.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Frame tariff policy risk as structurally sticky and multi-authority, not dependent on any single legal challenge succeeding.
insights.quote: This brick-by-brick reconstruction of the great tariff wall is nearing completion.
insights.locator: This brick-by-brick reconstruction of the great tariff wall is nearing completion

insights.claim: A 10 percent Section 122 tariff (a balance-of-payments remedy) was imposed after the Supreme Court ruling and is set to expire July 24, 2026.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term date to watch for either renewal, lapse, or escalation into the other tariff tracks already underway.
insights.locator: the administration imposed a 10 percent Section 122 tariff

themes.theme: New Section 301 Investigations Target Specific Countries

insights.claim: A Section 301 forced-labor investigation found sixty trading partners, including the EU and Japan, engaged in unfair trade practices; a 10-12.5 percent tariff has cleared its public comment period and could be imposed at any time.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A broad, imminent tariff action (60 partners) that markets may be underpricing given it has already cleared procedural review.
insights.locator: One Section 301 investigation recently found that sixty trading partners

insights.claim: A second, ongoing Section 301 investigation targets sixteen major trading partners — China, the EU, Singapore, Switzerland, Norway, Indonesia, Malaysia, Cambodia, Thailand, Korea, Vietnam, Taiwan, Bangladesh, Mexico, Japan, and India — for allegedly generating excess capacity that distorts fair competition, with results expected soon.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A wide-ranging investigation covering major electronics, auto-parts, and manufacturing exporters to the U.S.; a pending decision that could reprice trade flows across sixteen economies.
insights.locator: A second Section 301 investigation targeted sixteen

insights.claim: Froman questions whether the 'excess capacity' framework is analytically sound beyond China: there is consensus China deliberately over-builds capacity in sectors like solar panels and EVs to dominate export markets, but it is far less clear the other fifteen named countries pursue the same strategy or have comparable tools — raising the question of whether a mere trade surplus is being wrongly treated as proof of unfair competition.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Suggests the legal/analytical basis for tariffs on non-China targets in this investigation is weaker, raising the odds of successful trade-partner pushback or litigation.
insights.locator: This is a particularly interesting investigation

insights.claim: The administration imposed a fresh 25 percent Section 301 tariff on Brazil (with carve-outs for beef and orange juice) immediately after the Supreme Court invalidated a 50 percent IEEPA tariff on Brazil that had been tied to its treatment of former president Jair Bolsonaro.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A concrete case study of the administration re-imposing a struck-down tariff on the same target via a different legal vehicle within weeks — the template to expect for other IEEPA-invalidated tariffs.
insights.locator: just this Wednesday, the Trump administration announced a 25 percent tariff on imports from Brazil

themes.theme: Manufacturing Reality Lags the Tariff Rationale

insights.claim: Manufacturing's share of U.S. GDP has fallen since Trump's first term, from 10.7 percent in the first quarter of 2017 to 9.4 percent today, despite nearly a decade of escalating tariffs aimed at reindustrialization.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Hard evidence against the core stated rationale for tariff policy; useful benchmark for judging whether future tariff escalation is actually achieving its goal.
insights.locator: Manufacturing as a percentage of GDP has fallen since Trump’s first term

insights.claim: Manufacturing employment has been effectively flat since January 2025, and real wages for those workers are relatively stagnant, with tariffs contributing to the inflation eroding real pay.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: The jobs and wage-growth case for tariffs is not showing up in the data roughly 18 months into the current tariff regime.
insights.locator: manufacturing employment is effectively flat from January 2025 to date

insights.claim: Construction spending on manufacturing buildings is down 22 percent year-over-year (per the Wall Street Journal) even as data-center construction spending rises, partly because tariffs have raised construction material costs — suggesting the reshoring investment 'green shoots' the administration expects are not yet materializing.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A leading indicator (construction starts) that should show up before employment or GDP share data — its absence is an early red flag against the reshoring thesis.
insights.locator: the Wall Street Journal reported earlier this week that despite an increase in spending on data center construction

insights.claim: Froman frames the core trade-off as 'pay now, benefit (hopefully) later': tariff costs are immediate and visible while reindustrialization benefits, if they arrive, are delayed and uncertain, creating a real political challenge amid affordability concerns.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A useful mental model for judging the political durability of tariff policy: rising near-term cost visibility versus deferred, unproven benefit is a fragile political equilibrium.
insights.locator: That creates a real political challenge for the administration

insights.claim: The tariff-for-manufacturing strategy is not new to Trump's second term: it traces to 25 percent/10 percent Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs in his first term, was maintained and expanded by the Biden administration, and has been extended much further under the current administration.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Frames tariffs as a decade-long, bipartisan-continuity industrial-policy experiment that has yet to demonstrably deliver its stated manufacturing goal.
insights.locator: the experiment with raising tariffs as a means of driving increased manufacturing
